reasoning: The Lean statement faithfully captures the Putnam 2000 B5 problem. The sequence S : ℕ → Set ℤ with finiteness (hSfin) and positivity (hSpos) hypotheses matches "$S_0$ is a finite set of positive integers" — while these are stated for all n, they are automatically inherited from $S_0$ under the recursive definition (since $S_{n+1} \subseteq S_n \cup (S_n+1)$ preserves finiteness, and positivity is preserved since elements require $a \in S_n$ or $a-1 \in S_n \geq 1$). The recursive definition uses `Xor'` which encodes "exactly one of" exactly as `(A ∧ ¬B) ∨ (B ∧ ¬A)`. The "infinitely many N" phrasing is correctly encoded as `∀ n, ∃ N ≥ n, P(N)`, which for $\mathbb{N}$ is equivalent to unboundedness, hence to the set being infinite. The set $\{M : \mathbb{Z} \mid M - N \in S_0\}$ equals $\{N + a : a \in S_0\}$. All quantifiers, types, and conclusions match.
